data.title: First Northern Community Bancorp authorizes 6% buyback
Service Properties Trust prices $500M stock at $1.20 per share
KULR outlines plan to reach ~10,000 drone battery packs per month in H2 2026 as it targets gross margin improvement
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KULR Technology Group (KULR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • "2025 was a difficult year for our shareholders and for our company. Share price declined significantly, and we recorded a net loss of approximately $62 million." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Michael Mo) Mo added that "the majority of this loss was driven by onetime and noncash items" and said the 2026 focus is execution: "Our singular focus is to build and sell more KULR ONE batteries."
  • Mo highlighted 2025 items affecting reported results, including: "Under GAAP accounting, KULR recognized an unrealized mark-to-market adjustment of $13.8 million on its Bitcoin holdings for 2025" and "We have maintained our Bitcoin treasury of approximately 1,082 Bitcoins without selling any coins." He also said KULR "took the full write-off of approximately $6.9 million" tied to an exoskeleton investment after the private company "filed for insolvency," adding, "Clearly, this investment did not work out."
  • On growth priorities, Mo said 2026 performance will be judged by "core battery revenue growth and improvements in gross margin as volume and automation increase" and described steps to address "product sales gross margin of 1% in 2025," including: "we're installing an automated production line in second half of 2026" and more programs shifting from prototype to production.
  • Mo framed the commercial baseline for scaling: "Battery platform revenue, which is product sales plus contract services was $7.3 million in 2025." He contrasted that with total company revenue: "Revenue was $16.1 million, up 51%. Most of that growth came from Bitcoin mining and battery research grant dollars," while emphasizing "the number that matters most to us in 2026 is the battery platform revenue."
  • "KULR generated over $16 million in revenue in 2025. This is a 51% increase over the prior year." (CFO & Director Shawn Canter) Canter added mix shift detail: "Product revenue was up 39%, while service was down 50%."

Outlook

  • Mo pointed to a volume ramp tied to specific customer programs: "we're engaged with 2 of the leading unmanned aerial system companies in the United States with a combined production volume target to approach 10,000 packs per month in second half of 2026" and said the automated line is expected to support the ramp.
  • On timing by end market, Mo stated: "Hylio and Caban are both 2026 revenue contributors, Caban in production by now and grow for the remainder of 2026." He added, "we do expect that the Hylio revenue in second half of 2026," while "The AI data center BBU business... it will be more like a 2027 business for us."
  • For AI data centers specifically, Mo said 2026 work centers on standards and certification: "2026 is the year that we really need to work with our BBU cell providers on the UL 9540 certification and work with the hyperscaler customers on integration work."

Financial Results

  • Mo summarized the 2025 headline results and key drivers: "we recorded a net loss of approximately $62 million" and said a major component was noncash: "Under GAAP accounting, KULR recognized an unrealized mark-to-market adjustment of $13.8 million on its Bitcoin holdings for 2025."
  • Canter quantified noncash impacts: "There is an aggregate of approximately $33 million of noncash expenses on the income statement that contribute to the net loss. These represent almost 55% of it," and reiterated that "the largest of these is an approximately $14 million mark-to-market expense" related to Bitcoin price movement.
  • Canter described the 2025 revenue mix transition: "our product revenue increased and our service revenue declined," with product revenue sourced from "47 customers" and "Revenue per customer was approximately $108,000," while services revenue came from "34 customers" and "Services revenue per customer in 2025 was approximately $65,000."

Q&A

  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Which markets have the most momentum and repeat business? CEO Mo: "the KULR ONE Air for the autonomous platforms are the clearest near-term production momentum" and said KULR is "looking at over 10,000 battery packs per month later 2026."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Update on AI data center backup power and what to watch? CEO Mo: "2026 is the year that we really need to work with our BBU cell providers on the UL 9540 certification" and "I would say that 2027 is the year that we can see revenue opportunities."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Status in telecom/energy infrastructure and what needs to happen? CEO Mo: "We've taken control of the battery manufacturing equipment and process from Caban, and we've commenced production" and said consolidation into the Webster facility is planned "in Q2" to "improve efficiency to reduce costs."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Where will attention/resources go in the next year? CEO Mo: "The focus for 2026 is simple, build and sell more KULR ONE batteries" and "the management is most focused right now on the KULR ONE Air platform."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Biggest goals/milestones for 2026 success? CEO Mo: "the baseline is 10,000 packs per month as we get our automated production line going" while also calling out telecom volume shipping and potential Battery as a Service contracts.
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: How stable and repeatable is platform revenue becoming? CEO Mo: "We're certainly moving to a more stable and repeatable product sales business model in 2026."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Space-based AI data centers—any role? CEO Mo: "this is a long-term conversation, and it is not something KULR can focus on in 2026," adding, "there might be opportunities, but not particularly a focus for us in 2026."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: How much will Hylio/Caban/OCP initiatives contribute in 2026? CEO Mo: "Caban in production by now" and "we do expect that the Hylio revenue in second half of 2026," while AI data center BBU is "more like a 2027 business."
  • Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.: Aligning with drone makers increasing output? CEO Mo said automation is being set up "to be in operation in second half 2026" and added the "counter drone direct energy systems" battery "will go into production in 2026" alongside NDAA-compliant work with Hylio.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and opportunity-driven, with prompts like "The Caban announcement was a great start" (Stuart Smith, SmallCapVoice.com, Inc.) and repeated questions focused on what will convert into 2026 revenue vs. longer-dated opportunities.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was slightly negative on 2025 outcomes but forward-focused on execution, with Mo stating, "2025 was a difficult year" and emphasizing, "Our singular focus is to build and sell more KULR ONE batteries," while Q&A answers were consistent and execution-oriented around production ramps, certifications, and consolidation.
  • Versus the prior quarter, the tone shifted from Q3’s "strongest quarter to date" framing to explicit acknowledgment of shareholder impact and a large net loss in Q4, while still emphasizing product transition and scaling milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Mo’s messaging moved from Q3’s rapid scale ambition ("targeting 50,000 packs per month by mid-2026") to Q4’s more specific near-term ramp emphasis around "approach 10,000 packs per month in second half of 2026" tied to two U.S. unmanned aerial system customers.
  • The Q4 call placed heavier emphasis on reconciling reported losses and one-time items (Bitcoin mark-to-market and an investment write-off) than Q3, where balance sheet strength and Bitcoin strategy were more prominent and described in detail.
  • AI data center timing language became more deferred in Q4 Q&A, with Mo describing integration and UL certification work in 2026 and "2027" as the year for revenue opportunities, compared with Q3’s expectation that the BBU system would be "UL 9540 certified in production ready in 2026."
  • Telecom execution details advanced from Q3’s broader positioning to Q4’s operational updates: taking manufacturing control, commencing production, delivering packs in Q1 2026, and planning a Q2 consolidation into the Webster facility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Mo identified profitability and margin execution as a central risk area, noting "product sales gross margin of 1% in 2025" and tying improvement to volume scaling, automation in "second half of 2026," and a maturing modular platform to reduce onboarding engineering effort.
  • Mo highlighted non-operational volatility from digital assets, stating the company recorded "an unrealized mark-to-market adjustment of $13.8 million" in 2025 tied to Bitcoin price at year-end, while also stating KULR did not sell its "approximately 1,082 Bitcoins."
  • Capital allocation discipline was flagged after the exoskeleton investment loss: "We took the full write-off of approximately $6.9 million" and Mo said the lesson is to prioritize areas with "greater operational control" and "strong commercial visibility."

Final Takeaway

Management characterized 2025 as a difficult year marked by an approximately $62 million net loss, including significant noncash items tied to Bitcoin mark-to-market and a $6.9 million investment write-off, and positioned 2026 as an execution year focused on scaling the KULR ONE battery platform. The company repeatedly emphasized KULR ONE Air as the near-term production driver, citing active engagements and a production volume target to approach 10,000 packs per month in the second half of 2026, alongside automation and facility consolidation efforts aimed at improving efficiency and gross margin. Management also framed telecom (Caban) and NDAA-compliant drone work (Hylio) as 2026 revenue contributors, while describing AI data center BBU commercialization as more dependent on 2026 certification and integration work with revenue opportunity timing discussed as 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TSMC targets 2028 launch for 3nm chip production in Japan - report
S&P Global Australia PMI falls to 49.8, ending five-month growth streak
data.description:
The S&P Global Australia manufacturing PMI fell to 49.8 in March 2026 from 51.0 in February, indicating a decline in the manufacturing sector for the first time in five months.

Business conditions slightly worsened as the sector entered contraction.

"The Australian manufacturing sector suffered some of the effects of the war in the Middle East in March, most notably an intensification of inflationary pressures and disruption to supply chains," said Andrew Harker, Economics Director at S&P GlobalMarket Intelligence.

Separate data showed the Ai Group industry index for Australia’s manufacturing sector also decreased sharply to -27.9 in March, the largest monthly drop since April 2020.

Additionally, the Australian Industry Index dropped by 19.9 points to -23.6 due to the ongoing energy crisis, reversing its recovery in 2026.

Furthermore, the seasonally adjusted number of total dwellings approved in Australia surged 29.7% month-on-month to a near five-year high of 19,022 units in February 2026.

Private house approvals in Australia grew 0.2% month-on-month to 9,847 units in February 2026, preliminary estimates showed.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose to 8,615 around midday on April’s first trading session. The Australian dollar rose to around $0.692, rebounding from a two-month low.

More on Australia:

data.title: Japan's manufacturing PMI revised to 51.6 in March, topping initial estimates
data.description:
The S&P Global Japan Manufacturing PMI was revised up to 51.6 in March 2026 from an earlier estimate of 51.4. 

This reading decreased from a nearly four-year high of 53.0 in the prior month, as output and new orders slowed. Employment increased further to expand capacity and tackle ongoing labor shortages.

"The slowdown coincides with the outbreak of war in the Middle East, which, according to survey respondents, directly contributed to stronger cost pressures at the end of the first quarter. Notably, input prices rose to the greatest extent in over a year and a half, leading firms to raise their charges at a quicker pace as they sought to protect their margins. The war has also fueled greater uncertainty about the global economic outlook, dampening business confidence and resulting in more cautious hiring and purchasing activity," said Annabel Fiddes, Economics Associate Director at S&PGlobal Market Intelligence.

Separate data showed the Bank of Japan’s sentiment index for large manufacturers edged up to 17 in Q1 2026, beating market estimates of 16 and marking the highest level since Q4 2021.

The Nikkei 225 Index jumped 3.5% to above 52,800, while the broader Topix Index climbed 3.2% to 3,600 on Wednesday. The Japanese yen held its gains around 158 per dollar on Wednesday.

data.title: SpaceX readies 'Project Apex' mega IPO with 21 banks - report
data.description:
SpaceX (SPACE) is assembling one of the largest underwriting syndicates in recent years as it prepares for an upcoming IPO dubbed “Project Apex” that has lined up at least 21 banks, Reuters reported.

Morgan Stanley (MS), Goldman Sachs (GS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Bank of America (BAC) and Citigroup are expected to serve as lead bookrunners, while another 16 banks will participate in supporting roles across geographies and investor segments, the report added, citing people familiar with the matter.

Banks in addition to the active bookrunners, include Allen & Co, Barclays (BCS), Brazil’s BTG Pactual (BPAC), Deutsche Bank (DB), the Netherlands’ ING Groep (ING), Macquarie (MQBKY), Mizuho (MFG), Needham & Co, Raymond James (RJF), Royal Bank of Canada (RY), Societe Generale (SCGLY), Banco Santander (SAN), Stifel (SF), UBS (UBS), Wells Fargo (WFC), and William Blair. The plan is subject to change, and ​additional banks could ​still be added.

The Elon Musk-led company IPO is expected to launch as early as June 2026, with SpaceX targeting a valuation of roughly $1.75T, placing it among the most valuable companies globally upon listing. If the timing and pricing hold, 'Project Apex' would be a flagship U.S. tech IPO in 2026, with high leverage to Starship, Starlink’s cash‑flow ramp‑up, and NASA contracts.

Separately, Elon Musk denied excluding Robinhood Markets (HOOD) and SoFi Technologies (SOFI) from the SpaceX (SPACE) IPO.

data.title: RatingDog China PMI: Factory activity expands for fourth month despite missing forecasts
data.description:
The RatingDog China General Manufacturing PMI dropped to 50.8 in March 2026 from 52.1 in February, below the forecast of 51.6, indicating slower factory growth. 

Output, new orders, and employment increased, while production expanded for the fourth month, and backlogs grew.

On Wednesday, the Shanghai Composite rose 1.3% to 3,944, while the Shenzhen Component gained 1.2% to 13,639, and the offshore yuan stabilized around 6.88 per dollar on Wednesday, following significant gains in the previous session.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).

Currency: (CNY:USD)

data.title: NCino outlines fiscal 2027 free cash flow of $132M-$137M while targeting 10% ACV growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: nCino (NCNO) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “We exceeded our financial guidance across every key metric and delivered an exceptional ACV result, up 17% year-over-year,” with CEO Sean Desmond framing the outperformance as “largely driven by customers embracing our AI strategy and product innovation” (CEO, President & Director Sean Desmond).
  • AI commercialization and adoption was a core theme, with Desmond stating that “over 170 customers…have already purchased AI intelligence units as of the end of fiscal '26,” and adding, “we believe nCino is rapidly becoming the de facto AI platform for financial institutions across the globe” (CEO Desmond).
  • International and enterprise momentum were highlighted through deal callouts, including: “in the U.S. enterprise market, we delivered our best sales quarter in over 4 years,” a “marquee net new customer win in Austria,” and “the fourth quarter signing of one of the largest banks in the world for a commercial lending transformation” in Japan (CEO Desmond).
  • A key go-to-market change was announced: “nCino has hired Keith Kettell as our new Chief Revenue Officer,” as Desmond said the company is “further investing in our go-to-market organization” (CEO Desmond).
  • CFO Greg Orenstein reported Q4 financials and profitability expansion, stating, “Total revenues were $149.7 million,” “subscription revenues were $133.4 million,” and “non-GAAP operating income…was $34.7 million or 23% of total revenues” (CFO & Treasurer Gregory D. Orenstein).

Outlook

  • Q1 fiscal 2027 guidance was set at “total revenues of $154.5 million to $156.5 million” and “subscription revenues of $137 million to $139 million,” with “non-GAAP operating income…approximately $38 million to $40 million” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Based on the provided estimates, Q1 revenue guidance of $154.5 million to $156.5 million vs. $152,743,800.0 (analysts’ estimate). nCino did not provide EPS guidance in the current transcript, so no EPS comparison was included.
  • For fiscal 2027, nCino guided to “total revenues of $639 million to $643 million” and “subscription revenues of $569 million to $573 million,” and said, “Excluding U.S. mortgage, our guidance assumes 10% to 11% year-over-year subscription revenues growth” (CFO Orenstein).
  • On bookings/ACV, management guided to “net additions of $60 million to $65 million on a constant currency and organic basis,” implying “ending ACV to $662.5 million to $667.5 million, representing 10% ACV growth at the midpoint” (CFO Orenstein).
  • A guidance framework change was disclosed: “effective for fiscal '27, we will be providing annual guidance for free cash flow in lieu of quarterly and annual guidance for non-GAAP net income attributable to nCino per share” (CFO Orenstein).

Financial Results

  • Q4 results included “Total revenues…$149.7 million,” with “subscription revenues…$133.4 million” and “professional services revenues…$16.3 million” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Profitability and earnings were described as: “Non-GAAP net income attributable to nCino…was $42.8 million or $0.37 per diluted share,” and “free cash flow was $12.5 million” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Balance sheet and customer metrics included “cash and cash equivalents of $88.7 million,” “ACV as of January 31, 2026, was $602.4 million,” and “ACV net retention rate…increased to 112%” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Capital return and financing actions were emphasized: “We repurchased approximately 1 million shares…for total consideration of $25 million,” and “today we announced a $100 million accelerated share repurchase program,” alongside “a $200 million term loan expansion of our existing credit facility…funded by some of our largest customers” (CFO Orenstein).

Q&A

  • Alexander Sklar, Raymond James: Asked about close rates/win rates and pipeline coverage; CEO Desmond said “conversion rates were healthy, but we just had a larger pipeline,” and cited “discipline of just pipeline management overall” (CEO Desmond).
  • Alexander Sklar, Raymond James: Asked about where AI usage is strongest and credit allotments; CEO Desmond cited “agentic credit reviews,” “Locate and file,” and “credit monitoring,” and said nCino sold “large enough blocks of intelligence units upfront” to avoid customers feeling “nickel and dimed” (CEO Desmond).
  • Joseph Vruwink, Baird: Asked if AI orchestration threatens differentiation; CEO Desmond said “deploying that code…in a highly regulated industry…is still hard,” adding nCino is focused on “an Agentic operating system future” (CEO Desmond).
  • Michael Infante, Morgan Stanley: Asked about pricing transition and renewal pushback; CEO Desmond said nCino is “exceeding our internal plans and targets,” and that customers are “more focused on…outcomes…versus the old per user per seat model” (CEO Desmond).
  • Cristopher Kennedy, William Blair: Asked for ACV by category; CFO Orenstein replied, “we don't have that for this call” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Aaron Kimson, Citizens: Asked about CRO timing and sales execution; CEO Desmond said Paul Clarkson “is stepping aside for personal reasons” and that the CRO role helps nCino “scale to $1 billion and beyond” (CEO Desmond).
  • Charles Nabhan, Stephens: Asked about Sandbox and DocFox; CEO Desmond said Sandbox is “the foundation…of our integration gateway,” while DocFox is tied to “complex commercial onboarding,” with pipeline the company expects to “convert…in the first half of this fiscal year” (CEO Desmond).

Sentiment Analysis

  • “Slightly negative” to “neutral” analyst tone, with repeated probing on AI disruption risk, pricing pushback, and reconciling ACV-to-revenue mechanics, including questions on whether AI can “eat into nCino's differentiation” (Joseph Vruwink, Baird: Question) and why subscription revenue guidance does not align with ending ACV (George McGrehan on for Koji Ikeda, BofA: Question).
  • “Positive” management tone in prepared remarks, leaning into confidence language such as “we feel extremely confident about our position” and “we feel really good about where we are,” while responses to moat questions were assertive: “we believe our data moat is unparalleled and unique” (CEO Desmond).
  • Compared with Q3, management’s tone in Q4 added more emphasis on capital return and financing actions (ASR and term-loan expansion), while continuing the Q3 narrative that AI is creating a “halo effect” and driving “early renewals” (CEO Desmond; CFO Orenstein).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • AI adoption metrics increased from Q3’s “over 110 customers that have now purchased Banking Advisor intelligence units” to Q4’s “over 170 customers…having already purchased AI intelligence units,” alongside usage acceleration described as “banking adviser usage up over 25x in March compared to usage in October” (CEO Desmond).
  • Platform pricing penetration rose from Q3’s “approximately 27% of our ACV” to Q4’s “approximately 38% of our ACV away from seat-based pricing to platform pricing” (CEO Desmond; CFO Orenstein).
  • Q4 introduced a senior leadership change (new CRO) that was not present in Q3, and added a new reporting emphasis: fiscal 2027 free cash flow guidance replacing quarterly and EPS guidance (CEO Desmond; CFO Orenstein).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management directly addressed the market narrative that “AI will replace SaaS,” arguing banking is different due to “regulatory compliance,” “guardrails,” and the need for systems that are “traceable and auditable” (CEO Desmond).
  • On monetization pacing, CFO Orenstein cautioned that “financial institutions are going to adopt AI at a very deliberate pace,” and said fiscal 2027 guidance “does not yet contemplate” incremental bundles of intelligence units (CFO Orenstein).
  • Guidance risk language centered on deal timing variability: “these large deals can be inherently difficult to predict in both their timing and eventual sizing” (CFO Orenstein).

Final Takeaway

Management described fiscal 2026 as a year of improved bookings and retention tied to AI adoption, highlighted a move to platform pricing, and paired that narrative with material capital allocation actions, including a newly announced $100 million accelerated share repurchase and a $200 million term loan expansion. For fiscal 2027, leadership guided to $132 million to $137 million of free cash flow, 10% ending ACV growth at the midpoint, and reiterated a focus on reaccelerating subscription revenue growth while managing AI monetization expectations given “deliberate” customer adoption.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TruBridge expects modest 2026 revenue growth while anticipating 200 bps adjusted EBITDA margin improvement amid strategic review
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TruBridge (TBRG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Fowler framed Q4 and year-end results alongside two governance items, saying the company filed its 10-K after finding “certain out-of-period adjustments during final audit procedures with our new external auditor,” emphasizing “these adjustments are noncash and not material to our fiscal 2025 financial statements or to our previously issued financials,” and adding that TruBridge has been “engaged in a strategic review process considering a range of alternatives to maximize shareholder value.”

  • CEO Fowler said the strategic review is driving a change in disclosure posture: “As a result, we are not issuing formal guidance today, but we expect to achieve modest revenue growth in 2026 and anticipate approximately 200 basis points of improvement in adjusted EBITDA margins.”

  • CEO Fowler highlighted commercial momentum and mix, pointing to “bookings of $19.8 million on a total contract value basis,” support from “growing SaaS,” and “strategic partners, including Microsoft and our exclusive Dragon Copilot integration with TruBridge EHR,” while also calling out pipeline changes: “the dollar value of our overall sales pipeline is currently the highest it has been in 9 quarters and has increased 53% since the beginning of Q3.”

  • CFO Vinay Bassi said the audit work led to revisions: “we identified some material noncash misstatements primarily related to the timing of revenue for some products and associated contract costs, capitalized software and certain other nonroutine items,” adding, “We have revised these prior period financials to reflect them in the appropriate period.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated an informal 2026 framework rather than issuing guidance, with CEO Fowler stating, “we expect to achieve modest revenue growth in 2026 and anticipate approximately 200 basis points of improvement in adjusted EBITDA margins.”

  • CFO Bassi repeated the same directional outlook: “while we are not providing formal guidance due to our strategic review process, we remain confident that we can achieve modest revenue growth in 2026, along with continued adjusted EBITDA margin expansion of approximately 200 basis points.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, TruBridge moved from providing quarterly and full-year 2025 guidance plus an early 2026 margin expansion view to withholding formal guidance entirely; in Q3, CFO Bassi said, “we will not provide formal 2026 guidance until early next year as usual,” while also stating TruBridge believed it would deliver “margin expansion of around 200 basis points from the midpoint of our full year 2025 guidance.”

  • The analysts estimates provided were for fiscal 2026 quarters, but management did not provide EPS or revenue guidance figures for 2026 in the Q4 transcript, so no guidance-vs.-estimates comparison was available.

Financial Results

  • TruBridge reported Q4 revenue of $87.2 million and adjusted EBITDA of $19.2 million, with CEO Fowler stating revenue was “in line with the midpoint of the revised guidance we provided last quarter,” and CFO Bassi adding revenue was “a decrease of approximately 1% compared to a year ago,” while adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to “22% margin this quarter.”

  • Full-year 2025 results included revenue of $346.8 million and adjusted EBITDA of $68.7 million, with CEO Fowler adding free cash flow was $20 million and CFO Bassi stating cash flow from operations was $37 million.

  • Segment detail in Q4 showed Financial Health revenue of $56.2 million and Patient Care revenue of $31 million; CFO Bassi attributed Financial Health performance to “strong growth in the Encoder business,” while Patient Care reflected “a 6.6% year-over-year decline, primarily due to the sunset of our Centriq.”

  • Balance sheet and liquidity updates included CFO Bassi’s statement that TruBridge ended the quarter with $24.9 million in cash and reduced net debt to “approximately $139.8 million,” with “net leverage…2x,” alongside a financing update: “in November 2025, we entered into an amended and restated credit agreement…expires in 2030 with up to $250 million in credit facilities.”

Q&A

  • Sean Dodge, BMO Capital Markets: asked how to use the new ACV metric to assess visibility and later pressed on strategic review timing; CFO Bassi replied, “the recurring revenues and some assumption of bookings conversion…into revenue and attrition…is the right way of doing it,” while CEO Fowler said on timing, “we do not have a time line on this…more about getting to the right outcome as it is about hitting a certain deadline.”

  • Maxi Ma, Deutsche Bank: asked about bookings volatility, conversion timing, and capacity constraints; CEO Fowler said, “There are no capacity constraints,” and described booking timing as “a little bit at the mercy of the customer,” tied to replacement contract terms and facility onboarding dynamics.

  • Maxi Ma, Deutsche Bank: asked whether margin expansion is nearing a ceiling; CEO Fowler said, “I don't think we're at the end when we hit that 200. And I don't know where the ceiling is,” and CFO Bassi added, “revenue mix…will play as technology solutions like encoder keeps picking up.”

  • Jeffrey Garro, Stephens: asked about synergy from having Patient Care and Financial Health together and visibility into 2026; CEO Fowler pointed to “interconnectivity…with the rural community customer base with the EHR,” and CFO Bassi said, “We will continue to have some seasonality of some of the revenue streams and the timing of bookings.”

  • Sarah James, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked for detail and KPIs on AI to predict denials; CEO Fowler said TruBridge is “in a pilot format,” “still in a learning phase,” but outlined a target area: “probably 80%, 85% of claims are going through…So now there's an opportunity for 15% of the claims to be improved.”

  • Ryan Halsted, RBC Capital Markets: asked about a rural health fund and competitive dynamics; CEO Fowler said TruBridge is “100% locked in on helping our hospitals get into that $50 billion fund,” noted TruBridge was “selected by SAIC to be their preferred partner for the EHR and RCM technology,” and added on competition, “We have not seen that yet.”

  • Eugene Mannheimer, Prime Executions: asked about the RevSpring partnership impact timing; CEO Fowler said, “I don't think there's a meaningful impact this year,” adding, “we'll start to see some traction there towards the back half of the year and then moving into 2027.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on visibility (ACV-to-revenue translation), retention/attrition, conversion timing, margin durability, and strategic review timing; the most direct pressure point was the timeline question, while management repeatedly emphasized limits on disclosure.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks around pipeline, AI initiatives, and balance sheet progress, while sounding cautious on predictability and strategic review timing; CEO Fowler said, “we do not have a time line,” and described pipeline conversion as potentially “a quarter or 2 away from consistent quarterly performance.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, management added a more constrained posture on forward-looking detail due to the strategic review (no formal guidance), while continuing the confidence language on margins; Q3 included explicit 2025 guidance ranges and an early 2026 margin expansion view, whereas Q4 emphasized process, controls, and strategic alternatives.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • TruBridge shifted from issuing quarterly and full-year guidance in Q3 to withholding formal guidance in Q4, with CEO Fowler explicitly tying that change to the strategic review.

  • Q4 introduced two new stock-sensitive governance items absent from Q3’s framing: out-of-period adjustments tied to the first year with a new external auditor, and an ongoing strategic review “considering a range of alternatives to maximize shareholder value.”

  • Commercial narrative moved from Q3’s bookings disappointment and early-Q4 “strong start” commentary to Q4’s larger pipeline and mix assertions, including that the pipeline is “the highest…in 9 quarters,” that larger facilities increased to “30% of the pipeline,” and that “greater than 70%” of pipeline is recurring deals.

  • Analyst focus broadened in Q4, adding strategic review timing and the rural health fund opportunity, while continuing prior-quarter themes of bookings conversion and margin expansion drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged financial reporting and controls work as a near-term issue, with CEO Fowler noting “out-of-period adjustments” and CFO Bassi describing “material noncash misstatements,” while both emphasized they were noncash and tied to strengthening controls.

  • Customer retention remained a stated headwind, with CEO Fowler saying a “decrease in retention in our CBO customers” occurred as global teams adjusted, and that TruBridge is monitoring transition metrics such as “the cash in the door” and “communication with the customers.”

  • Management described uncertainty in the timing of bookings-to-revenue conversion as customer-driven; CEO Fowler said bookings were “at the mercy of the customer” due to replacement contract terms and operational onboarding factors.

Final Takeaway

TruBridge’s Q4 narrative centered on three investor-sensitive messages from management: the completion of a delayed 10-K after noncash revisions tied to a new auditor, the launch of a strategic review that is limiting formal guidance, and an operational plan built around modest 2026 revenue growth with about 200 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion. Management emphasized pipeline expansion, higher recurring mix, encoder momentum, and balance sheet progress, while acknowledging retention and customer-timed conversion dynamics as ongoing constraints on near-term consistency.
